channel.title: Publishing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Publishing on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Resurrecting The Arcanum IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about resurrecting the Arcanum intellectual property.

I get asked a lot on this channel in the comment section about making more Arcanum. And I have videos on doing a remake of Arcanum, You know, bringing it up to modern standards — hardware code, UI. There’s a video on.

I’ll link it below. I’ve also talked about remastering Arcanum. I’ve also talked about which.

item.title: Planning For A Sequel
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about planning for a sequel.

Okay, sorry about that. I » got a new microphone there. My voice is normal again, It’s got voice modulation and a bunch of fun stuff. It was a birthday gift from Robert, so I I won’t do that again, but I just want to have fun. And, yes, this video is going up on my birthday because the planning for a sequel question came from a master member. So it’s getting snuck into the queue, Otherwise it would have shown up in October, Anyway, matt P asked: “A new studio is building their first RPG with a brand new IP.

item.title: Game Rights
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about game rights, Specifically, who owns the rights to games that you’re playing and how the individuals who worked on those games, what they own and what they don’t own.

And, as usual, I want to talk to you first about why I’m doing this video, A frequently asked com question or comment that I get on this channel. I’m asked to review games and sometimes people couch it, as I just wanted you to give your thoughts on this game. It could be a game that has just come out, which is very frequent, a game that is similar to a game I’ve made in the past, or even sometimes people send me links to their own published games.

item.title: Adapting TTRPGs vs. Other IPs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about adapting tabletop role playing games into video games versus adapting a non-game IP into a video game.

I think the into the video game part should probably be implicit, because I’ve never adapted anything into anything else but a video game. So let’s talk about the pros and cons of having a tabletop RPG versus. Is a non-game RPG a non-game IP? This is an answer to a question from Dr John wubber, who asked: I’m curious, since you did adaptations of d and d and vampire, the Masquerade, but also South Park, what kinds of challenges did you find making a game based on another type of game versus making a game based on something non — interactive like a TV show? That is a really interesting question and no one has ever asked that before. It’s, by the way, that’s if you’re wondering, like what you can do. That’s a John’s question — is really interesting to me because it’s like: oh, I’ve literally done a video.

item.title: Working In Another's Game IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about working in another’s game IP.

This is not me talking about work making a non, taking a non-game IP and turning it into a game. I have a whole video on that. I’m talking about you’re making a game in someone else’s Game World — maybe it’s a, tabletop, and you’re converting it to a video game, which is a slight change of medium. Or maybe you’re making a sequel in someone else’s game world. Or maybe you’re working in a game where someone else is doing most of the design work. I want to talk about that because it’s an interesting feeling and an interesting process to be involved in something like that. But before I do, I want to tell you guys a story. It wouldn’t be a kanong games video, would it, without a story.

item.title: Using An IP In A Different Medium
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using an IP in a different medium, and

this is what I mean specifically is by IP is a license to an intellectual property. Maybe it was a tabletop role playing game, maybe it’s a book, maybe it’s a movie. For me, it would be making a game from that, and I have a whole video on converting movies into games, and there’s a link below, but it’s a similar process. This, using an IP in a different medium, is a similar process that TV shows or movies go through when they try to convert a book or game into their medium. And I’m not talking about the Fallout TV show here.

item.title: Converting A Movie Into A Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about converting, like a movie or TV show into a game.

I’m going to use an example. This is to answer a question from Andrew Williams, zn4, NP, who asked: uh, here’s a question idea. Have you ever watched a movie or show and thought that would make a fun game? If so, what movie or show would you do?

item.title: Romantasy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Romany, which is a new term that I learned and I will get into defining in a second.

But I want to give you some context. So I have a colleague — a work colleague — who on the side writes romance novels under, I believe, a pseudonym — I’m not sure — and I was really curious when I found this out, because I was asking them: you’re like why romance novels? And what they told me was it is one of the best selling genres of books, way outpacing other fiction genres like science fiction, fantasy, detective, you know novels and a lot of non-fiction as well. It’s just a very big genre and it apparently sells continuously. It doesn’t have Peaks for like around Christmas or summer, like I kind of thought that’s they were books that people kind of read in the summer. Nope, they apparently sell really well all the time and they’re fairly easy to write. So that, of course, made me think of the rise of romanceable Companions and RPGs, which I’m on record of saying.

item.title: Contractor Contracts
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about contractor contracts.

Now what I mean by that is I want to talk a little bit about if you’re thinking of doing Contracting — and I’ve only had experience in the game industry and not the Indie space at the game industry. I’m just going to talk about some things you may want to think about when you’re reviewing the contract. Now I’ve already talked about being a contract contractor, like the difference between doing contract work on a game versus being an employed member of the team on the game, so I’ll link that video below. I’m not going to talk about comparing a an employed member of the team versus being a contractor, and there are differences you should be aware of, so I recommend watching that video. I’ve also talked about publishing contracts, um, which are very different than contractor contracts. Publishing contracts are when you’re making a game for publisher. Those have things like royalties and breach of contract terms and monthly rates and a. Lot of things like that, and I talk about things to look out for in that video. So if you’re thinking about doing a game for a publisher, watch that one also.

item.title: Publisher Contracts
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about contracts, specifically the contracts you make with Publishers to make games.

And this is a response to TS 7901 who asked I would like to, who said I would like to hear more of the business side of your stories. So here we go now. Let me put a big caveat at the beginning of this. Bear in mind I don’t think I’m a good businessman. I do not think that I particularly ran the side of tra well and not like the accounting, paying bills side, but the dealing with Publishers and getting good contract side. So take a lot of this with a grain of salt, but also take it with uh from The View that I often think I learn more from failure than success and I pretty much failed here. So I know some things to look out for. But anyway, let me start by talking about Publishers. Publishers are not your enemy. I know a lot of you like to immediately take the employer bad, publisher bad, everything, bad side, so let me just say they’re not your enemy, they are your partners. You are trying to make a product with them.

item.title: Prequels
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prequels now I did a video on sequels and it’s linked below.

But since I did that, I’ve had a number of people ask me about whether I would like to do a prequel, whether what I think of prequels. Um, and I think it was memes Lich who was the first one who asked, I kind of had to dive back through the comments and he asked: what do you feel about pre prequels, Tim? Let me give you the TLD DW, since I think a lot of people DW. Um, my feelings on prequels are very similar to my feelings on sequels, but I do like them more than doing a sequel. I mean I’m not going to, you know, change my tune here.

item.title: An Example Of Making A New IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about making an IP an actual example.

Like I’ve talked about making and maintaining an IP, I’ve talked about the order to do it, in which is setting, story, system mechanics. I’ve talked about making what goes into a good setting. I’ve even talked about what your design doc should look like. Those are all videos I’ll link below.

Today, let’s do it. Let’s actually take something from one of my design idea books. I will talk about it, I will expound on it, I’ll show how the idea gets converted into an actual design that you can say now it’s an IP. So, and I will do it in the recommended order, talk about setting, story and systems. So my idea from this book was: I wanted to make a post-apocalyptic game — yeah, another post-apocalyptic game, but I didn’t want it to be nuclear war or any of the other things I’d use before. I had a new idea and what was going to be. Different about this one is: uh, a lot of people are gone, a lot of monsters, and I wanted to come up with more variation than what I got to do on Fallout, which was: here are some gos and giant bugs and mutants.

item.title: Troika And Kickstarter: What If?
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troa games and Kickstarter.

No, Kickstarter did not exist when tro was there, but someone asked a question on the channel. I think it’s been asked multiple times. It’s also something I’ve thought about a lot. It’s something I’ve actually discussed a little bit. I I know I had this discussion with Leonard I talked about with a couple other designers at obsidian when I was there. But let’s dive into it. Um, nu4, Ed — I don’t know how to pronounce — that asks: if Kickstarter had been a thing while tro was alive, would you have tried to crowdfund and if yes, what? So I will give you the answers as briefly as I can and then I’ll dive into it. Yes, and Arcanum 2, so yes, if, crowdfunding had existed, troa definitely would have gone that path. Tra was a very small company, when we made arcum, we were 14 people and because of that, we were very efficient.

item.title: Sequels
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today let’s talk about sequels.

I get a lot of questions about sequels, what I think about people making sequels, what I think about sequels that come out much later by other teams, um. So I thought I’d just talk about, like, my take on sequels, and I’ve said this, i’ I’ve mentioned this in other videos: in general, I don’t like making sequels, um, at least not nearly as much as making something original.

item.title: Piracy
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about piracy.

Dubie do 5000 asks: what are your feelings on video game piracy and anti-piracy DRM measures? Have your colleagues Express their own views to you on that topic? I’ll be honest, don’t really talk about piracy much anymore. I haven’t really talked about piracy with colleagues since gosh, I think, back in Fallout, when we were talking about whether to have DRM on the CD and I said that CD is full.

item.title: Game Pitches
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today let’s talk about game pitches.

Yes, I look like Ernest Hemingway. A friend said I looked like Ernest Hemingway with this beard, so I put on the turtleneck and got the pipe and I’m leaning into it.

But let’s talk about game pitches for real. So wikka fish ask: could you go into more detail about pitching a game? I’m curious as to what the process may look like, as well as the materials. You should have prototypes, storyboards, Etc. In order to have a successful pitch. That is a really good question.

item.title: Making And Maintaining An IP
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about making and maintaining an IP.

By IP, I mean intellectual property, now I’ve talked about writing design docs and how to make a good setting, so I’m going to link those two videos below. This is something different. I want to talk about intellectual property in general. Intellectual property is more than your setting. It’s more than your story. Those are specific to a game.

item.title: Troika's Might & Magic 10 Design
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about troika’s proposal from might Magic 10..

I’ll be honest, I’ve forgotten we had done this, but recently I did an interview with RAF colantonio and Peter salnikov on their YouTube channel, House of the dev — I’ll put a link in the description — and Ralph said that he always felt bad that Arcane was pitted up against Troika for might Magic 10.. You know, and I told him — I don’t even know if I knew that, but I told him, I mean, Publishers do that all the time. They like you know developers to compete for things. And I told him not to worry about it. We didn’t get it, but I went on to work on vampire anyway. This occurred right after Temple shipped.
